posts.title: 10 CSS life hacks that will make your websites better
posts.excerpt: Web development can be both challenging and exciting.
posts.content: Web development can be both challenging and exciting. Today we will share with you 10 useful CSS life hacks that will help you improve your websites. Use them for convenience and efficiency in your work!Flexbox for element arrangement: Use Flexbox to create flexible and responsive layouts without the need for complex CSS rules.Grid for creating grids: Use Grid to work with grids quickly and easily, allowing you to create complex layouts with ease.Hide elements with display: none: To hide an element on a page, set its style to display: none. This is useful for excluding content on mobile devices or during animations.Change the color of links: Set the color style for <a> elements to change the color of links on your website.Use opacity and visibility properties: Instead of setting display: none to hide elements, use opacity: 0 or visibility: hidden. This allows you to leave space on the page that can be occupied or animated.Compound selectors: Use compound selectors (e.g., .header h1) to select specific elements on a page.Transitions and animations: Add transitions and animations to your website to create seamless and engaging effects.Variable border width: Use the border-width property to create variable width borders that add depth and interest to your design.
posts.contentHtml:
Web development can be both challenging and exciting. Here are practical CSS techniques you can apply today to write cleaner, more maintainable styles.

1. Flexbox for layout

Use display: flex to build flexible, responsive rows without fighting floats:

.row {
  display: flex;
  gap: 1rem;
  align-items: center;
  justify-content: space-between;
}

2. Grid for two-dimensional layouts

When you need rows and columns, reach for Grid:

.cards {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(220px, 1fr));
  gap: 1rem;
}

3. Hide elements the right way

  • display: none — removes the element from layout
  • visibility: hidden — keeps the space, hides the content
  • opacity: 0 — perfect for smooth transitions
Prefer opacity and transform for animation — they run on the GPU and avoid layout thrashing.

Want to go deeper? Read the MDN CSS reference.

posts.title: Mastering the v-bind directive in Vue.js
posts.excerpt: There are many frameworks and libraries in the world of web development
posts.content: There are many frameworks and libraries in the world of web development, but one of the most popular and powerful is Vue.js. One of the key features of Vue.js is its system of directives that allow you to easily and efficiently manipulate DOM elements and their attributes. One of the most useful directives is v-bind.v-bind is a Vue.js directive that allows you to dynamically bind HTML attributes to data in your Vue.js application. It can be used to dynamically change the values of attributes, styles, classes, and other element properties.Here are some examples of how to use the v-bind directive:Dynamic attributes: Use v-bind to dynamically change the values of HTML attributes such as href, src, class, style, and others.Component parameters: Use v-bind to pass parameters to custom Vue.js components.Dynamic styles and classes: Change the styles and classes of elements on a page based on data in your application with v-bind.Manage element properties: Manipulate any HTML element property with v-bind, including disabled, readonly, validated, and more.Using v-bind allows you to create dynamic and interactive web pages that respond to changes in the data of your Vue.js application. This makes Vue.js one of the most popular choices for modern web development.Thanks to the v-bind directive, you can build powerful and efficient web applications with minimal effort. Master this skill, and your Vue.js projects will impress users.

posts.title: Understanding and Using the Component Lifecycle in Vue.js
posts.excerpt: In Vue.js, as in many other frameworks, each component has its own lifecycle
posts.content: In Vue.js, as in many other frameworks, each component has its own lifecycle, which consists of different phases that occur during its lifetime. Understanding this lifecycle is important for effective work with Vue.js and web application development. In this article, we'll look at the different phases of the component lifecycle in Vue.js and how to use them.beforeCreate: This phase occurs before the component instance is created. At this point, no component data has been initialized and no reactivity has occurred.created: After this phase, the component instance has been created and data is being initialized. At this point, you can make requests to the server or set up subscriptions.beforeMount: Before the component is inserted into the DOM. In this phase, you can perform additional preparations.mounted: When the component is inserted into the DOM. This phase is ideal for performing additional actions after the component has been fully rendered.beforeUpdate: Called before the component is updated, when data changes occur but are not yet reflected in the DOM.updated: After the component is updated and the corresponding changes are reflected in the DOM. This phase can be used to perform additional actions after the update.beforeDestroy: Before the component is destroyed. In this phase, you can remove subscriptions or clean up resources.destroyed: After the component has been destroyed and all subscriptions and resources have been cleared.

posts.title: JavaScript’s Secret Weapon: Supercharge Your Web Apps with Web Workers
posts.excerpt: During a recent interview, I found myself confronted with a question.
posts.content: During a recent interview, I found myself confronted with a question: How can we implement multi-threading in JavaScript? To my dismay, I was at a loss for an answer. JavaScript, by its nature, operates on a single thread. However, my curiosity led me to discover an intriguing solution: Web Workers.Web Workers represent a feature within JavaScript that unlocks the potential for concurrent script execution in web applications. They operate in the background, distinct from the main execution thread of a web page, thereby preventing UI blocking and enhancing responsiveness.This revelation was particularly enlightening for someone like me, previously unaware of this capability.Let’s explore a practical example to grasp the functionality and utility of Web Workers.To experiment with the provided code, it's essential to run a server; otherwise, the code won’t execute. The simplest approach is to globally install the http-server package via npm.

posts.title: Becoming a Hacker: Must Read Security & Cyber Crime Books
posts.excerpt: This book is at the top of the list because… well, the human element is the weakest link in any attack.
posts.content: This book is at the top of the list because… well, the human element is the weakest link in any attack.Think about it.How might your company get hacked? A security guard sitting at the front door, hired to keep unauthorized people out of the building, might let a “pest control guy” right in and even grant them access to the server room, as they’ve “been hired to get rid of the roaches”. The security guard really, really dislikes roaches, and doesn’t want to see any, nor does he want to have the chance of hitching some of them back to his home.Likewise, the receptionist may receive an email which looks to be from the CEO. It tells her to order a gift card in the amount of $500, and then send the card details over to him. Well, that wasn’t the CEO, it was a carefully crafted email written by a social engineer.That’s the basic idea of social engineering, it plays on people’s emotions and biases. If we arm ourselves with the knowledge of how they work, we’d be better able to guard ourselves, as well as the companies we work for.The author of this book, Christopher Hadnagy, also has some very engaging videos on YouTube explaining some of the concepts in the book. Check them out: 7 Jedi Mind Tricks: Influence Your Target without a Word, The Science Behind Human Hacking.The book is just as engaging as his talks.

posts.title: Powering Shopify’s High-Performance, PCI DSS v4 Compliant Checkout with Sandboxing
posts.excerpt: Navigating commerce compliance demands constant vigilance.
posts.content: Navigating commerce compliance demands constant vigilance. New regulations, such as the anti-skimming safeguards in PCI DSS version 4, pose significant challenges. Fortunately, merchants using Shopify's Checkout Extensibility feature can leverage Shopify's architecture to ensure PCI DSSv4 compliance.Shopify's Checkout architecture, powered by sandboxing, offers a robust solution for delivering a secure and compliant checkout experience. This architecture isolates custom code in sandboxed JavaScript environments, enhancing performance and security. Web Pixels for analytics and Checkout UI Extensions for additional content run within these sandboxes, communicating with the parent page via a mediated bridge.By utilizing sandboxing technologies, Shopify ensures that custom code runs independently of the main thread, maintaining performance and security standards. The architecture employs iframes and Web Workers to manage communication between the parent page and sandboxed code, ensuring a responsive checkout experience while mitigating security risks.The use of sandboxing is pivotal in achieving PCI DSS compliance, as it prevents the execution of custom code in the parent page. This approach guarantees a high-performance, low-maintenance, and future-proof checkout platform for merchants.PCI DSS version 4 introduces new requirements, particularly focused on anti-skimming protections. Merchants must maintain an inventory of all scripts, ensure only authorized scripts are loaded, and verify the integrity of each loaded script. Shopify Checkout, with its managed sandboxed runtime, streamlines compliance efforts for merchants, abstracting complexity and ensuring future-proof checkout experiences.With Shopify's proactive approach to compliance and security, merchants can focus on growing their businesses without worrying about regulatory implications. The managed runtime, coupled with robust security measures, provides a seamless and secure checkout experience, setting the stage for continued growth and success.

posts.title: Join us for the Netlify Dynamic Site Challenge: $3,000 in Prizes!
posts.excerpt: We're thrilled to announce our collaboration with Netlify
posts.content: We're thrilled to announce our collaboration with Netlify for the upcoming DEV challenge. This challenge is dedicated to crafting dynamic and high-performance digital experiences, regardless of the framework you choose!From now until May 12th, the Netlify Dynamic Site Challenge invites you to showcase your expertise, compete for cash prizes, and explore Netlify's cutting-edge Agnostic Platform Primitives. With these tools, you can unlock unparalleled functionalities across various frameworks like Next.js, Astro, Nuxt, Remix, and more.This challenge is a unique opportunity to contribute to the advancement of the open web and open-source community while honing your skills in emerging frameworks. We can't wait to witness the innovative projects you'll create.We encourage you to take on at least one of our three exciting prompts:Visual Feast:Create a dynamic image gallery leveraging the power of the Netlify Image CDN. Your project should harness Netlify's on-demand image transformation features, showcasing the ability to fetch images, apply transformations such as resizing and format optimization, and present the optimized images in an elegant gallery layout.For those ready to dive right in, here's the submission template. Remember to review all challenge rules on the official challenge page before submitting.Let's make this challenge a memorable journey of creativity and exploration!

posts.title: Improving the Online Store: New Theme Block Settings & Features
posts.excerpt: We're excited to announce the latest updates for your online store theme.
posts.content: We're excited to announce the latest updates for your online store theme, providing more control and flexibility for a better shopping experience. Key features include a custom countdown timer to create urgency, a dynamic announcement bar for key messages, flexible size grid metafields for customizable product information, enhanced mobile experience with optimized scrolling, and improved styling options for a polished look. Update your theme to the latest version to access these features and customize them from your Shopify admin panel. These updates aim to make your store more attractive and functional, enhancing the shopping experience for your customers. Explore the new features today and elevate your store!

posts.title: Mastering Container Units in CSS: Challenges, Solutions, and Best Practices
posts.excerpt: I experimented with using container units for every aspect of a design...
posts.content:
I experimented with using container units for every aspect of a design, expecting everything to scale seamlessly. Container units, which size elements based on their containers, seemed like a perfect solution. However, the reality was more complicated.Challenges with Container Units

One key issue is the difficulty in styling the container itself with container units. For example, applying consistent styles like border-radius can lead to unexpected results because different elements reference different containers.
Avoid Direct Container Styling

To avoid these pitfalls, it's often better not to apply size-based styles directly on the container. Instead, consider adding a wrapper element to maintain consistency in your design.
Text Sizing Issues

Using container units for text can cause it to become too large or too small, especially with varying card sizes. To ensure better control, mix container units with other relative sizing techniques, allowing for minimum and maximum size limits.
Layout Shifts

Container units are helpful for adjusting layouts at different breakpoints, but they can be tricky when switching between horizontal and vertical layouts. In these cases, using a combination of units often yields better results.
Conclusion

While container units offer strong scalability and flexibility, they aren’t a one-size-fits-all solution. Use them selectively, and combine them with other units to achieve more consistent and reliable designs.

posts.title: Netlify Aims To Simplify the Frontend With Cloud Primitives
posts.excerpt: Matt Biilmann, CEO of Netlify, highlighted at the InfoBip Shift conference in Miami that...
posts.content: Matt Biilmann, CEO of Netlify, highlighted at the InfoBip Shift conference in Miami that web development complexity is returning to the frontend, similar to what he observed in his original JamStack talk in 2016. Despite periods of reduced complexity, newer approaches are reintroducing it. Biilmann points to frameworks like Astro and Remix as solutions to simplify frontend development.Astro focuses on simplicity by using pre-generated static HTML, which suits content-driven sites with specific JavaScript interactions. In contrast, Remix emphasizes dynamic rendering with edge caching, integrating closely with CDNs like Netlify. Both frameworks offer different paths to reducing complexity while maintaining scalability. Netlify has partnered with Astro, committing $12,500 monthly to support its open-source community and development.

posts.title: Shopify Introduces FULFILLMENT_NOT_REQUIRED Status in Admin GraphQL API
posts.excerpt: Orders with no remaining items to fulfill will now return FULFILLMENT_NOT_REQUIRED instead of UNFULFILLED in Shopify API version 2026-10.
posts.contentHtml:
Shopify has introduced a new OrderDisplayFulfillmentStatus enum value called FULFILLMENT_NOT_REQUIRED in Admin GraphQL API version 2026-10. This update improves the accuracy of fulfillment status reporting and helps merchants and apps better understand the true state of an order.

What Is Changing?

Starting with API version 2026-10, Shopify may return FULFILLMENT_NOT_REQUIRED for orders that have no remaining items requiring fulfillment.

Examples include:

  • Orders that were fully canceled before fulfillment.
  • Orders that were fully refunded.
  • Orders where all line items were removed through order edits.

Previously, these orders were reported as UNFULFILLED, even though there was nothing left to fulfill.

Why This Matters

The old behavior could be misleading for both merchants and developers. An order marked as UNFULFILLED typically suggests that fulfillment work is still required. In reality, many canceled or refunded orders have no remaining fulfillable quantity.

The new FULFILLMENT_NOT_REQUIRED status makes this distinction explicit and provides a more accurate representation of the order lifecycle.

Developer Impact

If your application uses OrderDisplayFulfillmentStatus, you should review any logic that relies on specific status values.

Common areas to check include:

  • Status-to-label mappings.
  • UI badges and indicators.
  • Switch statements.
  • Order filters and reporting tools.
  • Generated GraphQL enum types.

For example:

switch (order.displayFulfillmentStatus) {
  case 'FULFILLED':
    break

  case 'UNFULFILLED':
    break

  case 'FULFILLMENT_NOT_REQUIRED':
    break
}

Backward Compatibility

This is an additive, backward-compatible change. Applications that already handle unknown GraphQL enum values gracefully should continue to function without modification.

API versions earlier than 2026-10 are not affected and will continue returning UNFULFILLED for these scenarios.

Key Takeaway

The new FULFILLMENT_NOT_REQUIRED status helps distinguish between orders that genuinely require fulfillment and orders that have no fulfillable items remaining. Developers adopting Admin GraphQL API 2026-10 should update their status handling logic to support the new enum value and ensure accurate reporting throughout their applications.

posts.title: Shop Minis Update: Product Variant Intents, Build Verification and New SDK Features
posts.excerpt: Shopify released a major Shop Minis update introducing product variant intent hooks, automatic build verification, new documentation, and deprecations.
posts.contentHtml:
Shopify has released the May–June 2026 update for Shop Minis, delivering new commerce capabilities, stronger submission quality checks, improved documentation, and SDK enhancements for developers building experiences inside the Shop app.

Product Variant Intents Arrive in the React SDK

The biggest addition in this release is support for typed product variant intent hooks. Developers can now use dedicated hooks for selecting variants, adding products to the cart, and launching express checkout flows directly from a Shop Mini.

The new hooks include:

  • useSelectVariant
  • useAddToCart
  • useBuyNow

When a Mini only knows the product ID, Shopify can automatically display the native Shop variant selector, allowing buyers to choose variants and quantities without leaving the experience.

const { addToCart } = useAddToCart()

const result = await addToCart({ productId })

if (result.code === 'ok') {
  switch (result.data.outcome) {
    case 'added_to_cart':
      break
    case 'navigated_to_product':
      break
    case 'redirected_to_product':
      break
  }
}

The API also provides detailed outcomes, making it easier to handle successful actions, user dismissals, navigation events, and product redirects.

Build Verification Is Now Part of Submit

Shopify has strengthened the submission workflow by integrating build verification directly into the submit command.

Before a Mini can be submitted, the CLI now performs multiple validation checks including:

  • Dependency validation
  • ESLint verification
  • TypeScript validation
  • Build output checks
  • Manifest validation

The verification process now provides clearer progress output and supports JSON results for automation and CI pipelines.

Improved Developer Experience

The CLI now warns developers when scoped SDK hooks are used before the required scopes or account configuration have been completed. This helps identify setup issues much earlier in development.

Shopify also expanded the list of allowed dependencies and added support for additional packages such as tw-animate-css.

Documentation Improvements

Several documentation updates were released alongside the platform changes.

  • New examples for useResolveIntent
  • Updated verify-build documentation
  • Improved submit workflow documentation
  • New guides for useProductReviews
  • Updated allowed dependency documentation

These changes make it easier for developers to discover platform capabilities and implement best practices.

Deprecation: usePopularProducts

Shopify has officially deprecated usePopularProducts. The hook now displays a development warning and should no longer be used in new Minis.

Recommended alternatives include:

  • useRecommendedProducts for personalized product discovery
  • useProductSearch for search-driven experiences

Package Versions

  • @shopify/shop-minis-platform — 0.26.0
  • @shopify/shop-minis-react — 0.28.0
  • @shopify/shop-minis-cli — 0.3.21

Why This Matters

This release focuses on three major areas: richer commerce interactions, stronger quality controls, and a better developer experience. Native variant selection simplifies shopping flows, automated build verification reduces deployment issues, and updated documentation helps teams adopt new capabilities faster.

For developers building inside the Shop ecosystem, this is one of the most significant Shop Minis updates of 2026 so far.

posts.title: Nuxt Team Is Developing an AI-Powered Nuxt Agent
posts.excerpt: The Nuxt team is actively developing Nuxt Agent, an AI-powered assistant integrated into the Nuxt documentation and ecosystem.
posts.contentHtml:
The Nuxt team is actively developing Nuxt Agent, a new AI-powered assistant designed to integrate directly with the Nuxt documentation and ecosystem.

What Is Changing?

Nuxt Agent is an ongoing initiative focused on bringing AI-assisted workflows closer to the framework itself. According to the Nuxt team, the agent is being integrated into the documentation and broader ecosystem to provide a more personalized development experience.

Why This Matters

Developers increasingly rely on AI tools while building applications. By integrating AI directly into the Nuxt ecosystem, the framework can potentially offer a more streamlined experience for discovering documentation, learning framework features, and navigating development workflows.

Developer Impact

At this stage, Nuxt Agent is still under active development. Developers should keep an eye on future Nuxt announcements for more details about functionality, availability, and supported workflows.

Key Takeaway

Nuxt Agent represents the Nuxt team's investment in AI-assisted development. While details remain limited, the project aims to bring AI closer to the Nuxt documentation and ecosystem to help personalize the developer experience.
